coredata.dc:title: “Two-in-one” sulfur and nitrogen co-doped fluorescent silicon nanoparticles: Simultaneous as the fluorescent probe and photocatalyst for in-situ real time visual monitoring and degradation of tetracycline antibiotics
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yi-Fan
Li, Zhi
Jiang, Ming
Yu, Xu
Xu, Li
coredata.dc:description: Detection and removal of contaminants are significant for environmental monitoring and remediation. In the present study, “two-in-one” silicon nanoparticles (SiNPs) were designed and prepared to simultaneously act as the fluorescent probe and degradation catalyst to detect and remove tetracycline (TCs) antibiotics. Thiourea and 3-aminopropyltrimethoxysilane were dopant and silicon source to generate fluorescent sulfur and nitrogen co-doped SiNPs (SN-SiNPs). The blue fluorescence of SN-SiNPs was selectively quenched by TCs due to the inner filter effect, whilst accompanied by the newly appeared yellow-green fluorescence resulting from aggregation induced fluorescence emission effect. Based on this phenomenon, SN-SiNPs can be used as fluorescent colorimetric probes for detection of doxycycline, oxytetracycline and tetracycline with limits of detection of 1.8 μg/L, 3.0 μg/L and 4.2 μg/L, respectively; the semi-quantitation can even be visually achieved by naked eyes. Particularly, SN-SiNPs were capable to catalyze the degradation of the three TCs effectively, achieving the removal rates of doxycycline, oxytetracycline and tetracycline of >90 %, >80 % and > 70 % after 240 min exposure to UV light. The catalytic ability of SN-SiNPs was derived from hydroxyl radical (•OH−), superoxide radical (•O2 −) and singlet oxygen (1O2) produced by SN-SiNPs under UV irradiation. Moreover, integrating the fluorescent probe and photocatalyst together, the proposed SN-SiNPs simultaneously realized catalyzing the degradation of the three TCs and in-situ visually monitoring of the degradation process in real time. This study innovatively proposed an integrated probe for the detection and catalytic degradation of TCs, providing a new “two-in-one” strategy for rapid and simple detection and removal of drug pollutants.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sulfur and nitrogen co-doped silicon nanoparticles
Tetracycline antibiotics
Colorimetric fluorescent probe
Catalyst
Environmental remediation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of TCs using SN-SiNPs as the probe
Catalytic degradation of TCs using SN-SiNPs as the catalyst
Detection of TCs using SN-SiNPs as the probe
Catalytic degradation of TCs using SN-SiNPs as the catalyst
Catalytic degradation of TCs by SN-SiNPs under UV light

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the financial support of this research by the Program for HUST Academic Frontier Youth Team (No. 2019QYTD09 ), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21804105 ), and Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities of China (No. 2172020kfyXJJS082 ). The authors also thank the Analytical and Testing Center of HUST for the material characterization.

The authors acknowledge the financial support of this research by the Program for HUST Academic Frontier Youth Team (No. 2019QYTD09), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21804105), and Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities of China (No. 2172020kfyXJJS082). The authors also thank the Analytical and Testing Center of HUST for the material characterization.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Effects of temperature (A) and reaction time (B) on QY of the fluorescent precursor. Effects of the doping amount of thiourea (C) and the amount of the fluorescent precursor (D) on the responds of SN-SiNPs to DOC. (25 μL of 20 mg/mL SN-SiNPs dispersion was mixed with 1.975 mL of 10 mg/L DOC aqueous solution, and the fluorescence intensity of the system was then measured as described in Section 2.3).

TEM image (A), size distribution (B), FT-IR spectrum (C) and XPS full scan spectra (D) of SN-SiNPs. High resolution XPS spectra of C 1 s (E), N 1 s (F), O 1 s (G), Si 2p (H) and S 2p (I) for SN-SiNPs.
The response of SN-SiNPs to different concentrations of DOC (A, D and G), OTC (B, E and H) and TC (C, F and I). (The experimental operation was described in Section 2.3).

(A) UV and fluorescence spectra of DOC and SN-SiNPs. (B) Fluorescence lifetime of SN-SiNPs and SN-SiNPs+DOC. (C) The fluorescence spectra of different materials before and after interaction with DOC. (D) Photographs of SN-SiNPs and SN-SiNPs+DOC under natural and UV light (λ = 365 nm).

(A) The removal rate of TCs under different conditions. (B) The relationship between the value of F510/F420 and the residual concentration of un-degraded TCs when SN-SiNPs acted as both the fluorescent probe and catalyst. The photographs of DOC (C), OTC (D) and TC (E) during degradation in the case of SN-SiNPs under UV irradiation (The experimental operation was described in Section 2.4).
(A) The effects of different chemical scavengers on the removal rate of DOC by SN-SiNPs. (BD) The ESR analysis results of SN-SiNPs. (The experimental operation was described in Section 2.4).
Schematic diagram of the synthesis process and “two-in-one” applications of SN-SiNPs.

head.ce:title: “Two-in-one” sulfur and nitrogen co-doped fluorescent silicon nanoparticles: Simultaneous as the fluorescent probe and photocatalyst for in-situ real time visual monitoring and degradation of tetracycline antibiotics

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Tongji School of Pharmacy, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan 430030, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Huazhong University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Tongji School of Pharmacy, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan 430030, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Detection and removal of contaminants are significant for environmental monitoring and remediation. In the present study, “two-in-one” silicon nanoparticles (SiNPs) were designed and prepared to simultaneously act as the fluorescent probe and degradation catalyst to detect and remove tetracycline (TCs) antibiotics. Thiourea and 3-aminopropyltrimethoxysilane were dopant and silicon source to generate fluorescent sulfur and nitrogen co-doped SiNPs (SN-SiNPs). The blue fluorescence of SN-SiNPs was selectively quenched by TCs due to the inner filter effect, whilst accompanied by the newly appeared yellow-green fluorescence resulting from aggregation induced fluorescence emission effect. Based on this phenomenon, SN-SiNPs can be used as fluorescent colorimetric probes for detection of doxycycline, oxytetracycline and tetracycline with limits of detection of 1.8 μg/L, 3.0 μg/L and 4.2 μg/L, respectively; the semi-quantitation can even be visually achieved by naked eyes. Particularly, SN-SiNPs were capable to catalyze the degradation of the three TCs effectively, achieving the removal rates of doxycycline, oxytetracycline and tetracycline of >90 %, >80 % and > 70 % after 240 min exposure to UV light. The catalytic ability of SN-SiNPs was derived from hydroxyl radical (•OH−), superoxide radical (•O2 −) and singlet oxygen (1O2) produced by SN-SiNPs under UV irradiation. Moreover, integrating the fluorescent probe and photocatalyst together, the proposed SN-SiNPs simultaneously realized catalyzing the degradation of the three TCs and in-situ visually monitoring of the degradation process in real time. This study innovatively proposed an integrated probe for the detection and catalytic degradation of TCs, providing a new “two-in-one” strategy for rapid and simple detection and removal of drug pollutants.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Sulfur and nitrogen co-doped silicon nanoparticles

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Water pollution is a growing serious public health problem with social development and human activities globally. Drugs have been regarded as one type of emerging water pollutants, as the residual drugs are claimed to have ecological risks and long-term cumulative negative effects on humans and ecosystem (Rani et al., 2021; Xu et al., 2021). For example, tetracyclines (TCs), a class of broad-spectrum antibiotics, were found to be ubiquitous in environmental waters as a result of the abuse and improper disposal of them (Xu et al., 2021). TCs are easy to accumulate in the environment due to high hydrophilicity and low volatility of them (Xu et al., 2021). Residual TCs in the environment may enter the human body through various ways, thus threatening human health, e.g. inhibiting the development of bones and teeth in infants and fetuses, damaging the liver and kidney because of deposition, and so on (Xu et al., 2021; Mejías et al., 2021). Unfortunately, traditional water treatment processes cannot completely remove the TCs, which leads to further accumulation of TCs in the environment (Michael et al., 2013). Therefore, monitoring and removal of TCs residues in water are of great significance, but still remain an enormous challenge. Up to now, a series of methods have been developed for detection and removal of contaminants (Long et al., 2022; Gao et al., 2020), while most of them can only be used for one single purpose, indeed, to detect or remove pollutants separately. However, in practical applications, detection and removal of contaminants complement each other. Detection is vital for the evaluation of pollution level; removal is the focus of environmental remediation, but the removal process and effect depend on detection for further evaluation. Thus, it would be appealing to develop an integrated technology that can simultaneously detect and remove TCs, and especially the removal process could be in-situ monitored in real time.

In recent years, researchers attempted using functional nanomaterials to achieve the goal (Long et al., 2022; Gao et al., 2020; Xiao et al., 2018; Zhao et al., 2020). For examples, Xiao et al. constructed a novel dual-function platform based on nitrogen-rich graphene-like carbon sheets for detection and degradation of bisphenols (Xiao et al., 2018). Zhao et al. prepared g-C3N4/AgNPs intercalated graphene oxide composite membrane via self-assembly approach, which was used for surface-enhanced Raman scattering detection and photocatalytic degradation of paraoxon-ethyl (Zhao et al., 2020). In these studies, the proposed nanomaterials were effective as both the detective probe and catalyst for detection and degradation of the pollutants. However, the detection and degradation were not really synchronous, but still separately processed, let alone in-situ monitoring the degradation process in real time. In addition, the tremendous instruments, like fluorescence spectrometer and surface enhanced Raman scattering instrument, were still involved in detection. For more cost-effective, time-saving and non-instrumental purpose, it is practical to develop the methods for visually in-situ detection and synchronously catalyzing the degradation of pollutants.

Fluorescent silicon nanomaterials have attracted extensive attention as fluorescence sensors due to their excellent optical properties, including adjustable fluorescence emission range, large Stokes shift and good photobleaching resistance (Miao et al., 2020; Oliva-Chatelain et al., 2016). More importantly, as one type of classical semiconductor materials, silicon nanomaterials possibly also hold great potential in light energy driven catalysis, owing to their short carrier collection paths and large surface-volume ratio (Liu et al., 2017; Shao et al., 2009). After being excited by light, silicon nanostructures can couple light energy into chemical reactions through various mechanisms, like photoexcited carrier transfer (Liu et al., 2017; Shao et al., 2009). However, less attention has been paid on the catalytic ability of fluorescent silicon nanomaterials. Supposedly, combining the dual properties of fluorescence and catalytic capability, fluorescent silicon nanomaterials would be promising as the multifunctional nanomaterials for simultaneous detection and catalytic degradation of pollutants, which was highly expected to meet the significant challenge for the integration of visually in-situ monitoring and synchronously catalyzing the degradation of pollutants.

Herein, sulfur and nitrogen co-doped silicon nanoparticles (SN-SiNPs) were prepared and attempted simultaneously as the detective probe and catalyst for detection and degradation of TCs. Unlike other SiNPs of similar size, the proposed SN-SiNPs synthesized by hydrothermal method using thiourea and 3-aminopropyltrimethoxysilane (APTMS) as raw materials were born with blue fluorescence. The blue fluorescence of SN-SiNPs was quenched by TCs as a result of the inner filter effect (IFE), while accompanied by new emergence of bright yellow-green fluorescence due to aggregation induced emission (AIE). In light of this phenomenon, SN-SiNPs were attempted as fluorescent colorimetric probes for detection of TCs, and further realized the visual detection. Meanwhile, SN-SiNPs could serve as a catalyst to accelerate the degradation of TCs under UV light, possibly owing to the derived hydroxyl radical (•OH−), superoxide radical (•O2 −) and singlet oxygen (1O2) originated from SN-SiNPs. Furthermore, the SN-SiNPs could catalyze the degradation of TCs along monitoring the degradation process in real time through the naked eyes. The schematic diagram of the synthesis and “two-in-one” applications of SN-SiNPs is depicted in Scheme 1 . The established method based on the fluorescent silicon nanomaterials creatively realized the integration of detection and removal in a real sense, i.e. the visually in-situ monitoring and synchronously catalyzing the degradation of pollutants, which would provide a new integrated platform for simultaneous detection and removal of environmental pollutants.

APTMS was from Hubei Wuda Silicone New Material Co. Ltd. (Wuhan, China). Thiourea, ethanol, NH4OH, tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), methanol (MeOH), tert-butanol (TBA), furfuryl alcohol (FA), 1,4-benzoquinone (p-BQ), spiramycin, roxithromycin, erythromycin, chloramphenicol, glucose (Glu), histidine (His), methionine (Met), glutathione (GSH), bovine serum albumin (BSA), lincomycin, nano TiO2 (anatase, 5–10 nm), NaCl, HCl and NaOH were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Doxycycline (DOC), tetracycline (TC) and oxytetracycline (OTC) were supplied from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ultrapure water was produced with a Heal Fore NW system (Shanghai, China).

Transmission electron microscopic (TEM) images were acquired on a Talos F200X microscope (FEI, Netherlands). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopic (FTIR) spectra were collected on an AVATAR 360 spectrometer (Thermo, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) measurements were performed with an ESCALAB Xi+ (Thermo Fischer, USA) using Al Kα radiation (hv = 1486.6 eV) as an X-ray source with a base pressure of 8 × 10−10 Pa. UV–vis spectra were recorded by a Lambda 365 UV–vis spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA). Fluorescence spectra were recorded by an F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). All the chromatographic separations were carried out on a Dionex Ultimate 3000 chromatographic system (California, USA) equipped with an eluent degassing device, two Ultimate 3000 pumps, an Ultimate 3000 automated injector, an Ultimate 3000 RS column compartment, and a diode array detector. The fluorescence lifetime decay curves were recorded by a Fluorescence Lifetime Spectrometer with Felix 32 system (Photon Technology International, Canada). The free radicals generated by SN-SiNPs were verified by Bruker Electron Spin (paramagnetic) Resonance Spectrometer (ESR, Bruker, Germany).

The preparation of SN-SiNPs followed two steps. Firstly, the fluorescent precursor was prepared by a simple hydrothermal method. Generally, 0.5 g thiourea dissolved in 4 mL ultrapure water and 1 mL APTMS were evenly mixed, and then transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave of 25 mL, and heated at 200 °C for 8 h. The resulting solution (1 mL), which was fluorescent precursor, was dispersed into 20 mL ethanol with stirring at room temperature for another 24 h to obtain uniformly dispersed SN-SiNPs. The product was thoroughly washed by ethanol and then dried for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of TCs using SN-SiNPs as the probe
ce:section.ce:para: Detection of TCs using SN-SiNPs as the probe was carried out in two ways, i.e. fluorescence measurement and visual observation. Briefly, 25 μL SN-SiNPs dispersion (20 mg/mL) and 1.975 mL of the individual TCs standard solutions, which were prepared in ultrapure water of specific concentrations (0, 0.005, 0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 5, 10, 30 and 50 mg/L), were incubated for the prescribed time. For the fluorescence measurement, these samples were subjected to the fluorescence intensity measurement at emission wavelengths of 420 nm (F420) and 510 nm (F510) excited at 365 nm. For the visual detection, these samples were subjected to UV excitation (λ = 365 nm), and the fluorescence color was observed and photographed with a smartphone. Thus, the standard fluorescence cards were established as the reference for visual semi-quantitation of the corresponding TCs.

For the linearity test, the linearity between the value of F420/F510 and the concentration of TCs was plotted. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated based on 3σ/k (σ was the standard deviation of F420/F510 without TCs, and k was the slope of the calibration curve).

For the selectivity experiment, 25 μL SN-SiNPs dispersion (20 mg/mL) was dispersed into each individual investigated substance aqueous solution (10 μg/mL), including DOC, OTC, TC, spiramycin, roxithromycin, erythromycin, chloramphenicol and lincomycin. The interference effect was evaluated with DOC as a representative. 25 μL SN-SiNPs dispersion (20 mg/mL) was dispersed into DOC solution (10 μg/mL) spiked with one of the other individual interfering substances (10 μg/mL), including Na+, K+, H2PO4 −, HPO4 2−, Glu, His, Met, GSH, BSA, spiramycin, roxithromycin, erythromycin, chloramphenicol and lincomycin. The fluorescence intensities of these samples were measured as mentioned above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic degradation of TCs using SN-SiNPs as the catalyst
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, the catalytic degradation of DOC using SN-SiNPs as the catalyst was compared under different lighting conditions. Briefly, 20 mL of 50 mg/L DOC standard solution and 5 mg SN-SiNPs were fully mixed and prepared in parallel for three portions, and each portion was separately subjected to dark, natural and UV light respectively. At the fixed time intervals (0, 10, 20, 30, 60, 120, 180 and 240 min), 1.0 mL suspension was sucked out from the system for immediate centrifugation (15,000 r/min, 10 min). The supernatant was then injected to HPLC for quantitation (Skúlason et al., 2003). Each experiment was carried out three times in parallel.

Catalytic kinetics experiments were carried out under UV light at room temperature. Generally, 20 mL of 50 mg/L TCs (DOC, TC or OTC) standard solution and 5 mg SN-SiNPs were fully mixed, and then subjected to UV irradiation. At the fixed time intervals (0, 10, 20, 30, 60, 120, 180 and 240 min), 2.0 mL suspension was sucked out and photographed under UV irradiation (λ = 365 nm) by a smartphone for the visual detection. One half of the sucked solution was then used for the fluorescence intensity measurement as described in Section 2.3, and the other half was centrifuged immediately (15,000 r/min, 10 min), the supernatant of which was injected to HPLC for quantitation. As a contrast, the catalytic degradation experiment with TiO2 as the catalyst was performed under the same conditions.

When SN-SiNPs acted as both fluorescent probe and catalyst, calibration was established as below. Taking HPLC as the standard method, the residual concentration of un-degraded TCs was accurately measured and calculated. With the residual concentration obtained in this way as the X-axis and the fluorescence value (F510/F420) at the corresponding time as the Y-axis, the relationship between the value of F510/F420 and the concentration were established. Thus, the degradation process could be monitored by fluorescence measurement. Meanwhile, these samples at the prescribed time intervals were subjected to UV irradiation (λ = 365 nm), and the fluorescence color was photographed with a smartphone. Thus, the standard fluorescence cards were established between the fluorescence color and the residual concentration measured by HPLC as the reference for visual semi-quantitation of the corresponding TCs during the degradation process.

The removal efficiency of TCs was calculated according to the Eq. (1), and the quasi-first-order kinetic model was used to calculate the catalytic rate (k) of TCs based on the Eq. (2).
(1)
Removal rate
%
=
1 −
C t
/
C 0
× 100 %
(2)
Ln
C 0
/
C t
= kt
C0 (mg/L) is the initial concentration of TCs, and Ct (mg/L) is the concentration of TCs at the time t (min).

In order to clarify the catalytic mechanism of SN-SiNPs, radical scavengers and ESR technology were adopted for this purpose. With DOC as the representative, KCr2O7 (Skúlason et al., 2003) was proposed to capture e−, TBA (Skúlason et al., 2003), MeOH (Chen et al., 2021), P-BQ (Matzek and Carter, 2017) and FA (Zhu et al., 2019) were employed as radical scavengers, respectively. Briefly, 20 mL of 50 mg/L DOC standard solution and 5 mg SN-SiNPs were fully mixed with the addition of each agent (KCr2O7: 25 mg/L, TBA: 500 mM, MeOH: 500 mM, P-BQ: 10 mM, FA: 10 mg/mL) separately, and then subjected to the UV irradiation. After 4 h, the concentration of residual DOC in each sample was determined by HPLC as described in Section 2.3. In addition, the ESR technique was employed to analyze the SN-SiNPs system under dark and UV light conditions, respectively.

As reported (Lin et al., 2016; Shi et al., 2019), some sulfur and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots were applicable to detect TCs based on fluorescence quenching as a result of the IFE. In addition, heteroatomic doping could render the material with catalytic properties due to electron transfer acceleration and other effects (Ding et al., 2018; Tripathi et al., 2020). Therefore, thiourea was herein selected as a raw material to provide S source. As another raw material, APTMS provided the SiO group, enabling the fluorescent precursor to form SN-SiNPs through simple dispersion with stirring under room temperature.

The synthesis of SN-SiNPs was divided into two simple steps. Firstly, the fluorescent precursor was obtained by hydrothermal reaction. Thiourea may release the ammonia under high temperature, which would facilitate the hydrolysis of APTMS. However, the hydrolysis of APTMS may be too fast to form flocculent substance. Thus, ethanol was further employed to help dehydration of the fluorescent precursors to produce the powder SN-SiNPs.

To obtain the best application performance, the synthetic conditions of SN-SiNPs, like the reaction time, reaction temperature and the added amount of thiourea, were optimized using the QY of the fluorescent precursor and the response of SN-SiNPs to DOC (F510/F420) as the criterion. The highest QY was generated at the reaction temperature of 200 °C (Fig. 1A) and time of 8 h (Fig. 1B), which were selected as the optimal reaction temperature and time. The response of fluorescent precursors prepared with different added amounts of thiourea to DOC (F510/F420) was investigated in Fig. 1C. With the increasing thiourea, the F510/F420 value exhibited parabolic variation, and the optimal amount of thiourea was 0.5 g. Afterwards, the ratio of fluorescent precursors to ethanol was investigated in Fig. 1D. At the fixed volume of ethanol, with the elevated fluorescent precursors, the F510/F420 value gradually increased until the added fluorescent precursors up to 1 mL, and then almost remained unchanged. Therefore, in the subsequent experiments, 1 mL of hydrothermal products were directly dispersed to 20 mL of ethanol with stirring to yield SN-SiNPs. In summary, the optimal synthesis of SN-SiNPs was performed as described in Section 2.2.

SN-SiNPs had a nanorod-like structure with the length and diameter of 150–270 nm and 60–90 nm, respectively (Fig. 2A, B). As disclosed in Fig. 2C, various functional groups, including -OH, -COOH and -NH2, may be present on the surface of SN-SiNPs (Ye et al., 2016; Sugimoto et al., 2016). The XPS measurement was further conducted to evaluate the elemental composition of the SN-SiNPs. In Fig. 2D of the full-scan XPS spectrum of SN-SiNPs, five obvious peaks at 284.8, 399.2, 532.1, 102.3 and 162.2 eV were observed, corresponding to C 1 s (53.83 %), N 1 s (11.92 %), O 1 s (19.62 %), Si 2p (11.27 %) and S 2p (3.35 %), respectively (Xing et al., 2020; Song et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2018). High-resolution spectra of each element were analyzed and illustrated in Fig. 2 E-I (Xing et al., 2020; Song et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2018), demonstrating the existence of C-OH, CO, CS and SiH in SN-SiNPs. Both the results of FT-IR and XPS spectra demonstrated the presence of hydrophilic functional groups such as -OH, -COOH and -NH2 on the surface of SN-SiNPs, which not only guaranteed the satisfactory water dispersion of SN-SiNPs, but also provided interaction sites for the detection of TCs.

The optical properties of the prepared SN-SiNPs were investigated by UV and fluorescence measurement. In Fig. S1A, the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of SN-SiNPs were 365 nm and 420 nm. The fluorescence stability of SN-SiNPs towards the surrounding NaCl was satisfactory, since the fluorescence intensity of SN-SiNPs remained almost constant in NaCl solutions of 0–1.0 M (Fig. S1B). As for the fluorescence stability of SN-SiNPs towards environmental pHs, the fluorescence remained stable between pH 5–10, but decreased slightly in strong acid (pH = 4) or strong base (pH ≥ 11) (Fig. S1C). The changes of fluorescence intensity may be related to the different ionization degrees of OH, -COOH and -NH2 on the surface of SN-SiNPs responding to different environmental pHs (Song et al., 2017). Anyway, the fluorescence stability of SN-SiNPs towards NaCl and pH (5–10) was satisfactory and appreciated in the subsequent applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of TCs using SN-SiNPs as the probe
ce:section.ce:para: In our preliminary experiment, once SN-SiNPs interacted with TCs, it was found that the blue fluorescence of SN-SiNPs at 420 nm was quenched by TCs accompanying with an appeared new emission peak centering at 510 nm; with the elevated concentration of TCs, the blue fluorescence of SN-SiNPs was further quenched while the yellow-green fluorescence was greatly strengthened. Hence, it was a ratiometric fluorescence system in light of only one external probe and the nature of the target, suitable for both fluorescence quantitation and visual semi-quantification of TCs.

In order to obtain the optimal conditions for detecting TCs using SN-SiNPs as the fluorescent probe, taking DOC as the representative target, the effect of pH (Fig. S2A), SN-SiNPs dosage (Fig. S2B) and incubation time (Fig. S2C) were studied using the F510/F420 value as the criterion. As depicted in Fig. S2A, the value of F510/F420 reached high and almost stable in the neutral environment (pH = 5–9), but became much smaller in both strong acidic (pH < 5) and basic conditions (pH > 9). This observation may be related to the changed ionization states of the surface functional groups of SN-SiNPs and DOC in different pH environments; at pH 5–9, the interaction between SN-SiNPs and DOC may be the strongest, resulting in the strongest responsive signal. Considering that the value of F510/F420 was almost irrespective of pH (pH = 5–9) and this pH range satisfied with most actual environmental samples (Long et al., 2022; Gao et al., 2020), the pH of the sample was not intentionally adjusted in the subsequent experiments.

Fig. S2B depicted the influence of the dosage of SN-SiNPs on the detection response, in which the value of F510/F420 first increased and then decreased with the increasing dosage of SN-SiNPs. The reason for this phenomenon was that the increasing dosage of SN-SiNPs at the initial stage guaranteed more availability of binding sites, enhancing the response to DOC (large value of F510/F420). However, when the dosage of SN-SiNPs enhanced too much, the blue fluorescence (F420) may strengthen to larger degree, while the fluorescence change caused by DOC was almost constant (strengthened F420 and weakened F510), and thus the value of F510/F420 conversely declined. Therefore, 0.25 mg/mL was selected as the optimal dosage of SN-SiNPs in subsequent experiments.

Fig. S2C investigated the incubation time required for the fluorescence to reach stable. Obviously, the response of SN-SiNPs to DOC reached stable in 15 s and remained constantly stable further, i.e. SN-SiNPs responded instantly to DOC, which was conducive to rapid detection. From the above analysis, when SN-SiNPs were adopted as the probe for TCs detection, the concentration of SN-SiNPs (0.25 mg/mL) was the only one parameter that needs to be controlled.

Under the optimal detection conditions, as displayed in Fig. 3A, B and C, F420 weakened and F510 strengthened with the elevated individual TCs concentrations from 0.005 to 50 mg/L. As plotted in Fig. 3D, E and F, the linearity between F510/F420 and TCs concentration could be obtained for DOC in the range of 0.005–30 mg/L (y = 0.746× + 0.650, R2 = 0.9911), OTC in 0.005–50 mg/L (y = 0.644× + 0.323, R2 = 0.9918) and TC in 0.005–10 mg/L (y = 0.469× + 0.347, R2 = 0.9945), respectively. The LODs were 1.8 μg/L, 3.0 μg/L and 4.2 μg/L for DOC, OTC and TC, respectively.

Although SN-SiNPs succeeded as the probe to determine the representative three TCs, the detectability of SN-SiNPs towards DOC, OTC and TC varied. It could be due to subtle differences in substituents (the position and number of hydroxyl groups) of these targets sharing the same parent structure, thus affecting their interaction with SN-SiNPs, such as hydrogen bonding, etc. (Buxton et al., 1988; Ji et al., 2016).

Corresponding to the elevated concentrations of TCs, the fluorescent color of the system transitioned gradually from blue to yellow-green, as photographed in Fig. 3 G-I. In this way, SN-SiNPs would be potential as a fluorescence colorimetric probe for visual semi-quantification of TCs. Fig. 3G-I were the established standard colorimetric cards for DOC, OTC and TC, respectively, for reference.

The selectivity and interference of SN-SiNPs as fluorescent colorimetric probe were investigated in Fig. S2D and E. Only TCs aroused the fluorescent color change from blue to yellow-green, while other tested antibiotics had no such effect, indicating that SN-SiNPs had good selectivity for TCs detection. In addition, from the Fig. S2E, when the studied interferences, including Na+, K+, H2PO4 −, HPO4 2−, Glu, His, Met, GSH, BSA, spiramycin, roxithromycin, erythromycin, chloramphenicol and lincomycin, coexisting with DOC were incubated with SN-SiNPs, ignorable fluorescence change was observed compared to the presence of only DOC and SN-SiNPs, demonstrating the satisfactory anti-interference performance of the proposed probe in detecting TCs.

The mechanism contributing to fluorescence color change of SN-SiNPs caused by TCs was explored with DOC as the representative. Firstly, as depicted in Fig. 4A, the UV spectrum of DOC and the excitation spectrum of SN-SiNPs overlapped significantly, which means that the fluorescence quenching of SN-SiNPs caused by DOC was probably attributed to the IFE (Lin et al., 2016; Shi et al., 2019; Jia et al., 2021), i.e. the absorption of excitation light by DOC weakened the fluorescence emission of SN-SiNPs. In order to further verify the fluorescence quenching via IFE, the fluorescence lifetimes of SN-SiNPs before and after DOC addition (SN-SiNPs+DOC) were compared in Fig. 4B. The results disclosed that the fluorescence lifetime was almost unchanged, which was consistent with the fluorescence quenching phenomenon via IFE (Shi et al., 2019; Ji et al., 2016).

The fluorescence emission spectra of DOC, SN-SiNPs and SN-SiNPs+DOC were recorded and compared in Fig. 4C. Obviously, DOC had nearly no fluorescence itself, and SN-SiNPs possessed blue fluorescence. A new strong emission peak appeared at ~510 nm for SN-SiNPs+DOC with the diminished blue fluorescence. In addition, the color and fluorescence of SN-SiNPs and SN-SiNPs+DOC under natural light and UV light (λ = 365 nm) were compared in Fig. 4D. It can be seen that the appearance color changed from white (SN-SiNPs) to yellow (SN-SiNPs+DOC) under natural light, while the fluorescence color changed from blue to yellow-green under UV light. Considering that DOC itself is a yellow crystal and contains tetraphenyl skeleton in the structure, it is speculated that the yellow-green fluorescence of the SN-SiNPs+DOC may come from the AIE effect; DOC was adsorbed onto SN-SiNPs and then aggregated by the interaction between DOC and the functional groups on the surface of SN-SiNPs, such as hydrogen bonding, etc. (Yu et al., 2019; Li et al., 2021).

To further explore the sensing mechanism of SN-SiNPs towards DOC, FT-IR and dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis of SN-SiNPs and SN-SiNPs+DOC were performed. Compared with SN-SiNPs (Fig. 2C), SN-SiNPs+DOC had no new absorption peaks in the FT-IR spectrum (Fig. S3), which was regarded as a simple combined spectrum of SN-SiNPs and DOC, suggesting no chemical reaction between them. DLS experiments revealed the mean size of SN-SiNPs of 563.0 nm, but much larger for SN-SiNPs+DOC, i.e. 700.3 nm, which may indicate the adsorption and aggregation of DOC on the surface of SN-SiNPs. Furthermore, which part of the probe was specific to TCs was preliminarily explored with pure silica nanospheres (SiO2), APTMS and thiourea alone as controls. As shown in Fig. 4C, no fluorescence enhancement similar to SN-SiNPs was observed when trying the three control substances as respective probes, proving that both thiourea and APTMS are indispensable as raw materials to realize the detection, and AIE of DOC may occur only in the response to SN-SiNPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic degradation of TCs using SN-SiNPs as the catalyst
ce:section.ce:para: It has been reported that polyatomic doping can improve the catalytic activity of nanomaterials (Ding et al., 2018; Tripathi et al., 2020). In our study, SN-SiNPs were found not only to be applicable to fluorescence colorimetric detection of TCs, but also to accelerate the degradation of TCs under UV light. Hence, the catalytic ability of SN-SiNPs was studied in detail.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic degradation of TCs by SN-SiNPs under UV light
ce:section.ce:para: According to the results in Section 3.3.1 (Fig. S2C), the adsorption of TCs on the surface of SN-SiNPs was almost instantaneous, i.e. the adsorption of TCs reached equilibrium instantly. Therefore, the catalytic degradation experiment could be carried out once TCs and SN-SiNPs dispersion were evenly mixed.

The effects of SN-SiNPs on degradation of DOC under dark, natural and UV light are compared (Fig. 5A). Obviously, SN-SiNPs did not show catalytic effect under dark and natural light, but significantly accelerated the degradation of DOC under UV irradiation, which implies the potential of SN-SiNPs as the UV catalyst for the degradation of DOC.

Under UV irradiation, the degradation process of DOC, OTC and TC with SN-SiNPs as the catalyst was monitored by HPLC (Fig. 5A). The results demonstrated that removal rates of DOC, OTC and TC reached >90 %, >80 % and > 70 % after 240 min exposure to UV. Although the degradation ability of SN-SiNPs to the three TCs was slightly different, the degradation of the three TCs can be divided into two parts, namely, the rapid stage of 0–30 min and the slow stage of 30–240 min. The different removal rates among these three TCs could be due to subtle differences in substituents (the position and number of hydroxyl groups), thus affecting their degradation kinetics (Pourmoslemi et al., 2016; Chen et al., 2021).

Moreover, the catalytic performance of prepared SN-SiNPs was compared with TiO2 (Fig. 5A), taking DOC as an example. It can be seen that, although the degradation of DOC also contained a rapid stage (≤ 30 min) and a slow stage (> 30 min) with TiO2 as the catalyst, the degradation of TCs catalyzed by SN-SiNPs was faster than that of TiO2. It took about 240 min to achieve DOC removal of 90 % in the case of SN-SiNPs as the catalyst, while it needed 480 min with TiO2 as the catalyst to achieve the same removal level. From this point, SN-SiNPs was more effective than TiO2 when catalyzing the degradation of TCs.

The catalytic mechanism under UV irradiation is usually related to the free radicals produced through the absorption of photons by the catalyst (Chen et al., 2021). Considering that K2Cr2O7 and TBA can effectively capture e− and hydroxyl radical (•OH−) (Chen et al., 2021), MeOH is a quencher of •OH− and sulfate radical (•SO4 −) (Matzek and Carter, 2017), P-BQ can remove •OH− and superoxide radical (•O2 −) (Buxton et al., 1988), and FA can specifically remove singlet oxygen (1O2) (Zhu et al., 2019), K2Cr2O7 was proposed as electron capturer, and TBA, MeOH, P-BQ and FA were selected as radical scavengers to investigate their effects on the degradation of DOC catalyzed by SN-SiNPs, respectively (Fig. 6A). In the presence of K2Cr2O7, the removal of DOC was significantly inhibited (with the inhibition rate of ~89 %), suggesting the existence and importance of e− in the degradation process. The inhibitory effect of TBA and MeOH on the removal of DOC was similar at less degree (with the inhibition rate of ~14 %), indicating the presence of •OH− while the effect of •SO4 − was negligible. The P-BQ significantly inhibited the removal of DOC (with the inhibition rate of ~74 %), suggesting that •O2 − was largely involved in addition to •OH− in the catalytic process. Besides, FA also significantly inhibited the removal of DOC (with the inhibition rate of ~45 %), indicating the presence of 1O2 in the catalytic system. Based on the above analysis, SN-SiNPs could generate •OH−, •O2 − and 1O2 under UV irradiation, and thus catalyze the degradation of TCs.

The existence of these free radicals was further confirmed by ESR analysis. As shown in Fig. 6B-D, no obvious signal was observed in the system without UV illumination, suggesting that free radicals could not be generated under dark conditions. However, under UV irradiation, significant signals of •OH− (Fig. 6B), •O2 − (Fig. 6C) and 1O2 (Fig. 6D) appeared, signifying their generation from SN-SiNPs. The results of ESR test were consistent with those of the above radical scavengers experiments, demonstrating that the catalytic capability of SN-SiNPs was attributed to •OH−, •O2 − and 1O2 produced under UV irradiation.

It has been reported that the synergistic effect of polyatomic doping can instantly provide high-density active sites (Ding et al., 2018; Tripathi et al., 2020), and the strong interaction between heteroatomic doping center and functional groups of material surface can also effectively improve the catalytic performance due to the acceleration of electron transfer (Ding et al., 2018; Tripathi et al., 2020). In addition, it has been proved that hydrogen atoms in SiH can act as electron traps to accelerate the separation of electron-holes, and form Si-OH bonds after reaction (Shao et al., 2009; Liu et al., 2017). Both FT-IR and XPS results in Section 3.2 proved the existence of SiH in SN-SiNPs, which would favor the catalytic ability of SN-SiNPs. Besides, the characterization in Section 3.2 disclosed abundant functional groups on the surface of nanosized SN-SiNPs. The nano size gave SN-SiNPs a large specific surface area, and abundant functional groups provided favorable catalytic and binding sites, both of which were conducive to its catalytic performance.

As discussed in Section 3.3 and 3.4, SN-SiNPs possessed the potential as both the fluorescent probe and photocatalyst. Inspired by the dual functions of SN-SiNPs, simultaneous catalyzing the degradation of TCs and in-situ monitoring the degradation process in real time was attempted.

Firstly, the dual roles of SN-SiNPs as the fluorescent probe and catalyst simultaneously were explored, as described in Section 2.4. The relationship between the value of F510/F420 and the residual concentration of un-degraded TCs could be established in Fig. 5B. Using this relationship as the calibration curve, during the catalytic process, the un-degraded concentration of TCs could be accurately obtained through the fluorescence measurement in real time. Through the fluorescence measurement, the kinetics of degradation of three TCs could be obtained, the same as those obtained by HPLC (data not shown).

Moreover, visual monitoring of the degradation process was investigated. Corresponding to the sampling time intervals, the photos were taken under UV irradiation in Fig. 5C-E. With the degradation going on, the yellow-green fluorescence gradually weakened and transitioned to blue, indicating that the degradation of TCs can be intuitively judged by the simply visual observation. Via the comparison of the fluorescence color of the degradation system and the standard fluorescence cards (Fig. 5C-E), semi-quantitation of the residual concentration of TCs could be roughly estimated. In other words, SN-SiNPs were efficient as the catalyst for degradation of TCs under UV irradiation and the fluorescent colorimetric probe to monitor the degradation degree of TCs at the same time, integrating in situ detection and catalytic degradation in one.

In this study, SN-SiNPs with blue fluorescence were obtained using thiourea as dopant and APTMS as silicon source. The blue fluorescence of SN-SiNPs could be selectively quenched by TCs, along with the appearance of bright yellow-green fluorescence, so SN-SiNPs was successful as a fluorescent colorimetric probe for fluorescence detection of TCs. In addition, SN-SiNPs were effective as the catalyst to accelerate the degradation process of TCs under UV irradiation. The catalytic mechanism of SN-SiNPs was attributed to •OH−, •O2 − and 1O2 generated in the system under UV irradiation. The silicon-based nanomaterials synthesized in this study realized the integration of colorimetric detection and catalytic degradation of TCs, which was promising for simultaneously catalyzing the degradation of TCs and in-situ real time monitoring the degradation process visually. This study innovatively integrated the detection and catalytic degradation of antibiotics, providing a new “kill two birds with one stone” strategy for rapid detection and removal of environmental pollutants.
Yi-fan Wang: Methodology, Writing original draft preparation & Editing
Zhi Li: Methodology
Ming Jiang: Data analysis
Xu Yu: Writing draft preparation, Funding acquisition
Li Xu: Supervision, Funding acquisition

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support of this research by the Program for HUST Academic Frontier Youth Team (No. 2019QYTD09), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21804105), and Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities of China (No. 2172020kfyXJJS082). The authors also thank the Analytical and Testing Center of HUST for the material characterization.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fig. S1 UV and fluorescence spectra (A) and the fluorescence stability of SN-SiNPs to NaCl (B) and pH (C). Fig. S2 The effects of pH (A), SN-SiNPs amount (B) and incubation time (C) on the response of SN-SiNPs to DOC. The selectivity (D) and interference (E) of SN-SiNPs as the fluorescent probe for detection of TCs. Fig. S3 The FT-IR spectra of DOC and SN-SiNPs+DOC.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2022.157470.
